Does the SSIM term fix the blur? — refiner analysis

Generated in-session · frozen-L1 (job 51876098, ep95) · frozen-SSIM (51950141, ep26) · joint-L1 (51876099, ep34) · breathing-val protocol, n=60 unless noted.

TL;DR. The refiner has two jobs: fill dark/dim spots (✅ both losses do this — big PSNR gain) and remove in-plane blur (the hard one). On the controlled frozen comparison (same seed, same frozen geometry, only the loss differs): the 2D-SSIM term is active and costs no accuracy — it pays a tiny PSNR tax early then ties L1 at matched steps. On sharpness, SSIM reaches the ceiling faster (0.692×GT by epoch 26 vs L1 needing ~ep62), but both plateau at the same modest ~0.69×GT ceiling — only ~8% of the gap to GT. So SSIM is a faster, no-cost improver, not a blur silver bullet: the splat itself is the real bottleneck. SSIM is still young (ep26 vs ep95) — the decisive matched-epoch verdict comes when it reaches ep50/ep95.

1. The two failure modes, and why L1 alone wasn't enough

The reconstruction is built by splatting the input slices into a canonical cube (V_canon), then a small 3D-UNet refiner cleans it up (V_refined). Two distinct defects:

  • Black / dim spots — voxels with little or no input coverage come out dark. A per-voxel intensity loss fixes these well (just predict the missing brightness).
  • Blur — the splat's trilinear scatter + coverage division throws away high-frequency detail; the heart edges come out soft. Earlier analysis (_html/08) attributed ~75% of the blur to the splat renderer itself.

Plain L1 is mean-seeking: the loss-minimizing output is the average of all plausible textures, which is smooth. So L1 brightens the holes but has no incentive to produce crisp edges — it fixes spots, not blur.

2. How the SSIM term works

We add a structural-similarity term to the refiner loss (applied to V_refined only; the geometry's L1 supervision stays untouched):

L_post = 1.0 · L1(V_refined, V_gt)  +  0.1 · (1 − SSIM2D(V_refined, V_gt))
  • SSIM compares local structure & contrast in an 11×11 window, not per-pixel values. It rewards matching GT's edges/texture — exactly the high-frequency signal L1 ignores.
  • It is two-sided / reference-based: it penalizes blur (too smooth vs GT) and hallucinated or misplaced edges (sharp where GT is smooth). It can't be gamed by just cranking up contrast.
  • 2D per-slice, in-plane: each axial (Y–X) slice is scored independently ((B,D,H,W)→(B·D,1,H,W); the through-plane Z is never mixed). The cube is anisotropic (8 mm Z over 12 slices vs 1.4 mm in-plane) and the blur we fight is in-plane, so 2D is the right choice — a 3D window would mix the coarse Z axis.
  • L1 stays in the mix because SSIM is contrast-normalized (blind to a uniform brightness offset); L1 nails absolute intensity, SSIM nails structure. (Recipe per Zhao et al. 2017.)

3. Is the SSIM term actually doing something? (it is)

The frozen-L1 and frozen-SSIM runs share the same seed and the same frozen backbone + point head, so their splat V_canon is byte-identical (26.41 bbox / 19.07 motion in both). If the SSIM weight were 0 they would be bit-identical at every step. They aren't — confirming the term is live:

Refined PSNR at matched steps. SSIM (red) dips slightly below L1 (blue) early — the expected "PSNR tax" from optimizing structure instead of pure MSE — then crosses over around step 25k. Net: SSIM costs no accuracy.

4. Does it fix the blur? — sharpness

Sharpness = in-plane gradient energy of the reconstruction ÷ that of GT (1.0 = as sharp as GT). The raw splat sits at 0.668.

Left: both refiners lift sharpness to ~0.69×GT — a small gain (the dashed green line is GT). Right: but SSIM gets there faster per epoch — 0.692 by ep26, where L1 needed ~ep62 for 0.688. The catch: both seem to be converging to the same ~0.69 ceiling.

Key finding. SSIM's structural pressure genuinely bites — it sharpens faster and at no PSNR cost. But the absolute sharpness ceiling (~0.69×GT) looks shared with L1, because the splat discards detail neither loss can invent. The refiner can only recover what's still latent in [V_canon, coverage].

5. Black spots ✅ vs blur ❌ — scorecard

Both losses close most of the dark-spot gap (large PSNR gain, left) but only ~8% of the blur gap to GT (right). This is the core story: dark spots — fixed; blur — only marginally improved, by either loss.

metric (n=60)raw splatL1 @ep95SSIM @ep26
sharpness / GT0.6680.6940.692
bbox PSNR (dB)26.4928.1627.69
motion PSNR (dB)19.1420.2619.91
training epoch—9526

Note the epoch gap: L1 is 3.6× more trained. SSIM's lower current PSNR is mostly immaturity, not a deficit — at matched steps they tie (§3).

6. See it — qualitative panels

Same subject, same input, same V_canon (frozen geometry) — only the refined row differs by loss. Compare the bottom two rows to GT.

Zoomed heart crop (edge crispness)

Both refined outputs are visibly cleaner than the raw splat (dark-spot/coverage fixed), but neither recovers GT's crisp myocardial edges — the residual blur both losses leave behind.

7. The joint run (for context)

The third run (joint-L1, ep34) co-trains the aggregator + point head with the refiner, so its geometry/V_canon differs (higher PSNR base 26.51, but lower sharpness base 0.623 at ep22 → 0.651 refined). It's mid-training and not directly comparable to the frozen pair, but tracking healthily. It tests whether letting geometry co-adapt helps — a separate axis from the SSIM question.

8. Verdict & next steps

  • Black spots: solved by the refiner (either loss).
  • Blur: only marginally improved. SSIM helps a bit more and a lot faster, but both losses hit a ~0.69×GT ceiling set by the splat.
  • SSIM is worth keeping — faster convergence, no PSNR cost, and it may still edge ahead once mature.
  • Decisive test pending: compare SSIM @ep50 vs L1's saved checkpoint_50 (matched epoch) and again at ep95.
  • If blur is the priority, the real lever is upstream: fix the splat/coverage (higher-res scatter, learned splat, or a coverage-aware loss), or add a gradient-matching term — because the information SSIM needs to sharpen further isn't in the splat anymore.
